title: U.S. Navy Year in Review
description: As the U.S. Navy confronted the demands of ongoing naval combat and the need to reset the fleet for great power naval competition, 2025 was another year of near-constant action.
text:
As the U.S. Navy confronted the demands of ongoing naval combat and the need to reset the fleet for great power naval competition, 2025 was another year of near-constant action. Balancing those imperatives remains the greatest challenge for Navy leaders.
The year featured naval operations in the eastern Mediterranean and Red Seas that eventually led to direct U.S. air and missile strikes against Iran’s nuclear facilities. The Navy’s presence in the Pacific increased, including more exercises with allies and partners. The Navy also ramped up presence in support of joint U.S. operations in the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific, culminating in the capture of Venezuela’s president. This is the new normal for U.S. Navy operations in the 21st century.
New Leaders
As overseas operations continued apace, the Navy’s top echelon completely changed after the Trump administration assumed office in January. First, in February, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth relieved Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) Admiral Lisa Franchetti, and Vice Chief of Naval Operations Admiral James Kilby served as acting CNO for five months. John Phelan, head of a private investment firm, was confirmed by the Senate in late March as the 79th Secretary of the Navy.1 Admiral Daryl Caudle, a career submariner, was nominated as the Navy’s 34th CNO and confirmed by the Senate on 31 July.2
In his first message to the fleet, Phelan pledged to revitalize U.S. shipbuilding using aggressive improvements, ensure sailors and Marines get world-class training, pursue excellence and innovation, and equip U.S. naval forces with “the best weapons on earth.”3 Since assuming office, CNO Caudle has issued a series of CNO Notes (C-NOtes) to demonstrate progress in implementing his “Foundry, Fleet, Fight” vision for the Navy. Like CNO Admiral Elmo Zumwalt’s famous Z-grams in the early 1970s, Caudle’s notes are intended as yardsticks to grade progress in achieving his new vision for the fleet. “These will be grounded in my vision, informed by your feedback, and driven by operational necessity,” he explained.4
Combat Operations
The Dwight D. Eisenhower Carrier Strike Group (CSG) participated in U.S.-led air strikes during Operation Poseidon Archer that led to the Houthis briefly ceasing attacks on ships following a 15 January ceasefire agreement between Israel and Hamas.5 The Harry S. Truman CSG launched its first strikes against the Houthis on 8 January 2025, hitting weapon storage facilities in Yemen. In February, the Harry S. Truman CSG’s Carrier Air Wing (CVW) 1 made naval aviation history in terms of bomb tonnage, when 16 F/A-18 Super Hornets dropped 124,000 pounds of ordnance against Islamic State operatives in Somalia in less than two minutes.6
A lull in naval operations permitted the Harry S. Truman (CVN-75) to conduct an emergent repair availability at U.S. Naval Support Activity Souda Bay, Greece, on 16 February, following her collision with a civilian tanker in Port Said on 12 February.7 The Harry S. Truman CSG resumed operations in time for the 15 March start of Operation Rough Rider, the U.S. response to the Houthis’ declaration that they intended to resume attacks on shipping in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. Operation Rough Rider destroyed Houthi radar, air-defense, and missile systems.8 The Harry S. Truman CSG conducted near-continuous operations, launching air strikes and Tomahawk land-attack missiles (TLAMs) against the Houthis for more than 50 days, until the United States announced a ceasefire on 6 May.9
While the overall number of Houthi attacks fell in the spring ahead of the May ceasefire, Navy ships on several occasions still had to defend against attacks. On 28 January, the destroyer USS Gravely (DDG-107) used her last-ditch close-in weapon system (CIWS) to intercept a hostile missile.10 On 26 April, the Harry S. Truman engaged incoming missiles and drones with both her 5-inch gun and CIWS.11 Also in April, while conducting evasive maneuvers to avoid a medium-range ballistic missile, the Harry S. Truman lost overboard both a Super Hornet and a tow tractor.12 And in May, the cruiser USS Gettysburg (CG-64) mistakenly launched Standard Missiles (SM-2s) at a pair of Super Hornets returning to the Harry S. Truman, resulting in the loss of one F/A-18F. Both crew members ejected safely.
While the Navy performed well during this extended operational engagement, the expenditure of hard-to-replace missiles and munitions was troubling. The Navy estimates that more than 220 missiles and 150 5-inch shells were fired during 380 engagements.13 To address this lopsided cost-to-performance ratio, Navy leaders are changing tactics and procedures to employ a different mix of options to defeat drone and missile threats, including jamming, air-launched weapons, and less expensive shipboard systems.14
The United States launched Operation Midnight Hammer on 22 June to destroy Iran’s underground nuclear facilities. A Navy Ohio-class guided-missile submarine, believed to be the USS Georgia (SSGN-729), and the aircraft carriers USS Nimitz (CVN-68) and Carl Vinson (CVN-70) were deployed to support the U.S. Air Force’s B-2 Spirit stealth bomber strikes on Iran’s hardened nuclear facilities. The Georgia is believed to have launched more than two dozen TLAMs at the Natanz and Isfahan nuclear facilities to degrade Iranian air defenses. The Nimitz and Carl Vinson air wings contributed to more than 125 U.S. aircraft sorties providing escort, protection, and intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance support for the B-2s.
Operations Southern Spear and Absolute Resolve
Navy ships returning from deployments to Europe, the Mediterranean, and the Red Sea diverted to the Fourth Fleet area as part of a significant naval buildup for counternarcotic operations. Navy ships operated across the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific Ocean and conducted a maritime blockade on sanctioned oil tankers operating near Venezuela. In what would eventually be announced as Operation Southern Spear, the USS Gerald R. Ford (CVN-78) and her air wing (CVW-8), Winston S. Churchill (DDG-81), Bainbridge (DDG-96), and Mahan (DDG-72) deployed to Fourth Fleet. The Gerald R. Ford CSG joined the USS Iwo Jima (LHD-7), Fort Lauderdale (LPD-28), and San Antonio (LPD-17) with the 22d Marine Expeditionary Unit embarked, along with the USS Lake Erie (CG-70), Gettysburg, Stockdale (DDG-106), Thomas Hudner (DDG-116), and Gravely in the Caribbean.
U.S. Coast Guard and Marine Corps personnel operating from Navy ships had boarded several sanctioned oil tankers by 9 January 2026. By the end of December, 34 alleged drug-smuggling boats had been destroyed in the Caribbean and eastern Pacific, resulting in an estimated 115 deaths.15
Months of preparations culminated in Operation Absolute Resolve to capture Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro. On 3 January, helicopters of the U.S. Army’s 160th Special Operations Aviation Regiment took off from U.S. Navy ships to capture Maduro at the Miraflores Presidential Palace in Caracas. More than 150 aircraft, some from the Gerald R. Ford and Iwo Jima, hit multiple targets in the vicinity of Caracas, the Fuerte Tiuna military base, the presidential palace, and La Carlota Air Base within a span of 30 minutes. Maduro was subsequently flown to the Iwo Jima for follow-on transport to the United States to stand trial.
Global Naval Exercises
With the Russia-Ukraine war entering its third year and China conducting military exercises around Taiwan—notably its large-scale Strait Thunder 2025A and Justice Mission 2025 exercises simulating a blockade of Taiwan—the Navy’s numbered fleet commands undertook a series of exercises to enhance maritime security and interoperability with allies and partners.16
Seventh Fleet exercises included Exercise Sea Dragon 2025, a multinational antisubmarine warfare training exercise with Australia, India, Japan, and South Korea; Pacific Steller, in which the French carrier Charles de Gaulle and Japan’s aircraft-carrying multirole cruiser Kaga operated with the Carl Vinson in the Celebes and Philippine Seas; and a medical evacuation, search and rescue, and visit, board, search, and seizure (VBSS) exercise with 11 Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) members in the ASEAN-U.S. Maritime Exercise cohosted with Indonesia.
In Sixth Fleet, Commander, Task Force 66, conducted exercises with robotic and autonomous systems (RASs) during Dynamic Messenger 2025, Baltic Operations, and a mine countermeasures (MCM) exercise in Sea Breeze 2025. Ships from the Gerald R. Ford CSG integrated with the Italian Navy frigate Spartaco Schergat beginning on 20 July and led an allied maritime force with Allied Maritime Command and NATO allies Norway, Germany, and France to operate in the High North from 23 August to 8 September.17 The Navy’s Fifth Fleet exercises included more than 30 nations in MCM, VBSS, and harbor security. The smaller Intrinsic Defender exercise with the Israeli Navy in the Mediterranean and Red Seas focused on VBSS, diving, and maritime/air domain awareness.
Hybrid Fleet Takes Off
The Navy’s efforts to modernize the fleet include a rapidly developing RAS and unmanned systems force. Fleet experimentation with RASs and unmanned systems is growing. Navy leaders have discussed for more than a decade the central role of RASs and unmanned systems in a hybrid fleet. New Navy plans, coupled with growing fleet testing, exercises involving larger unmanned formations, and a sharp increase in industry investment in unmanned systems and technologies, have brought the unmanned vision to reality.
In September, the Secretary of the Navy issued a memo to CNO Caudle and the Commandant of the Marine Corps calling for a new program acquisition executive (PAE) for RASs. “To ensure our warfighters operate with the most capable unmanned systems available, we must organize with purpose and invest in mission-critical autonomy,” Secretary Phelan wrote.18 PAE RAS brings together, for the first time, a majority of the Navy’s disparate RAS and UxS programs into the new acquisition construct.19 The new office, led by Rebecca Gassler, will consolidate 47 separate efforts spread across more than a dozen Navy and Marine Corps program offices.20 PAE leaders will have greater authority to make tradeoffs within their portfolios, reallocate funding between programs to accelerate program breakthroughs, or stop programs that are faltering.
The PAE RAS follows the standing up of the Navy Rapid Capabilities Office (NRCO), which Secretary Phelan announced in August. NRCO absorbs several existing entities, including NavalX, the Maritime Response Rapid Capability Cell, the Disruptive Capabilities Office, and Navy efforts related to the former Replicator initiative. NRCO’s writ is to find and field new operational solutions to meet urgent fleet needs on a three-year timeline.21 Congress also added $1.2 billion to the Navy’s budget in the reconciliation bill for small unmanned surface vessels (USVs).
The fleet also is increasing the number of unmanned systems in experimental and operational scenarios. In April, the Navy announced the standup of a second USV squadron for small USVs, USVRon 7 in San Diego, to be equipped with the Global Autonomous Reconnaissance Craft (GARC). The new squadron joins sister USVRon 1, based in Ventura County, California, and USVRon 3, also in San Diego.
The Navy’s Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Fleets all expanded their testing and operational integration of unmanned systems over the past year. The Bahrain-based Fifth Fleet has long championed innovative technologies, and the command was an early adopter of small unmanned systems, primarily for gathering data on “pattern of life” activities across the Red Sea. In 2025, Fourth Fleet launched an ambitious effort that shifted USVs from experimentation to real operations. Using up to 20 Saildrone USVs, the objective is to integrate them directly into the long-running Operation Southern Spear. “Southern Spear will continue our [Fourth Fleet’s] move away from short-duration experimentation into long-duration operations that will help develop critical techniques and procedures in integrating robotic and autonomous systems into the maritime environment,” said Commander Foster Edwards, Hybrid Fleet Director at Fourth Fleet.22
The Navy’s growing interest in unmanned systems is being matched by a wave of industry investment in smaller shipyards and in rapidly developing industrial manufacturing capacity to design, build, and field both USVs and unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs). New entrants to the naval shipbuilding sector include companies such as HavocAI, Saronic, Blacksea Technologies, and Blue Water Autonomy.
Huge investments from venture capital firms are turbocharging their unmanned plans. Blacksea, for example, is building dozens of GARCs per month and was the first firm to garner a construction contract for small USVs as part of the Pentagon’s accelerated acquisition initiative.23 Blue Water Autonomy has partnered with Conrad Shipyard to build a USV designed to meet the Navy’s Modular Attack Surface Craft requirement.24 Finally, the Navy awarded Saronic Technologies a contract valued at more than $392 million to produce its Corsair small USV. The firm, now valued at more than $4 billion, also announced its future Port Alpha production hub, where it plans to build additional types of USVs.25
Building the Golden Fleet
The start of 2025 and President Donald Trump’s return to the White House brought a renewed effort to revitalize U.S. shipbuilding and the U.S. Navy with a “Golden Fleet” shipbuilding plan. The plan includes next-generation warships such as guided-missile battleships and a tentative request for at least 49 new auxiliary, support, and transport ships in the 2026 budget request.26
The goal of increasing fleet size was revealed through several 2025 shipbuilding initiatives. A formal Office of Shipbuilding was established in the White House to oversee shipbuilding across the entire government and ensure compliance with overall national security and economic goals. The White House also issued an executive order, the Maritime Action Plan, that authorized federal agencies to invest directly in logistics infrastructure and shipbuilding facilities.
Congress reintroduced the SHIPS for America Act to help fund a commercial fleet of 250 U.S.-built and -flagged vessels. Korean firm Hanwha announced a $5 billion infrastructure investment plan at Philly Shipyard (now Hanwha Philly Shipyard), which it acquired in 2024, to increase annual ship construction to 20 units.27 With a significant boost from Congress, the Navy’s requested fiscal year (FY) 2026 shipbuilding base budget of $20.8 billion was bolstered by an additional $26.5 billion in the reconciliation bill, for a total of $47.3 billion. The added funding secured construction for one Virginia-class submarine, two Arleigh Burke–class destroyers, 43one America-class amphibious ship, one San Antonio–class amphibious transport dock, nine medium landing ships, and two fleet replenishment oilers. These ships were in addition to the Navy’s original budget request for one Columbia-class submarine, one Virginia-class submarine, and one ocean surveillance ship.28
Essential to any plan to increase fleet size, the Navy continued its recruiting surge, surpassing its 40,600 enlistment goal by nearly 9 percent in 2025, with 44,096 recruits.29 The strong recruitment results, some of the Navy’s best in more than two decades, were complemented by the Navy reenlisting a total of 34,347 active-duty sailors, meeting its retention goals across all zones.
In addition to operational and marketing changes, increased recruiter manning, and digital outreach aimed at Generation Z, the Navy facilitated recruiting by raising the maximum recruit age to 41, removing educational requirements, and lowering the minimum Armed Forces Qualification Test score to the 10th percentile.30 It is unclear if recruitment and retention in 2026 will be affected by the broad prohibition on beards, restoring the pre-COVID requirement for officers and sailors to undergo two physical fitness assessments each calendar year, or administratively separating personnel from the Navy following three fitness assessment failures in four years.31
The High-Medium-Low Fleet
The vision for the Golden Fleet began to take shape in the final weeks of 2025. Where Admiral Elmo Zumwalt’s 1970s high-low shipbuilding strategy called for smaller, low-cost ships to augment larger, high-price ones, the Golden Fleet appears to be adding larger, more capable battleships and a large number of USVs with containerized weapon systems to augment the costly fleet of Arleigh Burke–class destroyers. In effect, the Golden Fleet is a high-medium-low shipbuilding strategy that provides the Navy with ships large enough to carry hypersonic missiles, while maintaining the flexibility to support the weapons and equipment required by the manned fleet.32
At the high-end of shipbuilding in the Golden Fleet, the future USS Defiant (BBG-1) would displace 15,000–20,000 tons, a marked departure from the Navy’s current preference for smaller, more numerous surface ships. If built, these battleships would be the Navy’s first attempt to build a large surface combatant since canceling 29 of the 32 planned 15,907-ton Zumwalt-class destroyers. While it would displace less than Russia’s two remaining 24,300-ton Kirov-class nuclear-powered battlecruisers, the future battleships would be larger than the 13,000-ton Renhai-class cruisers China is building.
Although a battleship would add more large, highly capable ships to the fleet, the bulk of the fleet and ships under construction will still consist of existing amphibious, destroyer, and submarine classes in service and now under construction. The USS Iowa (SSN-797) was the most recent Virginia-class submarine to join the fleet, commissioning on 5 April. The USS Utah (SSN-801) was christened in October, and the Massachusetts (SSN-798) and Idaho (SSN-799) were delivered to the Navy in November and December, respectively. The bow section of the USS District of Columbia (SSBN-826), the first-in-class SSBN to replace the Ohio class, was completed, bringing the boat to 60 percent complete as of October. Still, it remains 12–16 months behind schedule, with delivery now anticipated for FY 2028.33
Surface ship deliveries in 2025 were paltry. The final Independence-class littoral combat ship, the USS Pierre (LCS-38), was commissioned on 15 November, and the commissioning of the future Cleveland (LCS-31), the final Freedom-class LCS, is now delayed until spring 2026. Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) Shipbuilding launched the future USS Jeremiah Denton (DDG-129), and Ingalls and Bath Iron Works each laid the keel for an additional DDG-51. Finally, the USS John F. Kennedy (CVN-79) is expected to begin sea trials in early 2026.34
In October, the Navy announced plans for HII Newport News Shipbuilding and General Dynamics Electric Boat to build 10 Block VI Virginia-class attack boats and five Columbia-class ballistic-missile submarines.35 In January, the Navy named the first three Block VI Virginia-class boats: the future USS Potomac (SSN-814), Norfolk (SSN-815), and Brooklyn (SSN-816). Block VI boats are the second set of Virginia-class submarines to be built with the Virginia Payload Module. This capability meets the Navy’s requirement for a land-attack missile platform following the decommissioning of the four Ohio-class guided-missile submarines by 2030.36 At least one of the Block VI boats is expected to be built as a specialized variant to replace the Seawolf-class USS Jimmy Carter (SSN-23).37
Decommissionings and Cancellations
The inactivation and decommissioning of older ships continued in 2025, with a similarly inconsistent schedule as 2024’s.38 The Los Angeles–class submarines USS Helena (SSN-725) and Key West (SSN-722) were decommissioned in July and September, respectively. Other Los Angeles–class submarines slated for decommissioning—the USS Pasadena (SSN-752) for 2025 and the Topeka (SSN-754) and San Juan (SSN-751) for 2024—remain commissioned. The USS Philippine Sea (CG-58) and Normandy (CG-60) were decommissioned in September 2025, but the Shiloh (CG-67) and Lake Erie (CG-70) remain active despite their proposed 2025 decommissioning dates. All four remaining Harpers Ferry–class and six of the remaining Whidbey Island–class dock landing ships remain in commission status despite prior plans for decommissioning in the 2021–25 timeframe. Four Military Sealift Command Spearhead-class expeditionary fast transports (EPFs) also were removed from active service in 2025.
The USS Dextrous (MCM-13) and Gladiator (MCM-11) were decommissioned in Manama, Bahrain, leaving the four remaining Avenger-class MCMs in Sasebo, Japan. The year saw the long-awaited first operational deployment of the MCM mission package on board the USS Santa Barbara (LCS-32) and Canberra (LCS-30). The LCSs equipped with MCM packages will eventually replace all the remaining crewed MCM ships in the fleet. “We are changing the way we’ve done business over the years, and it’s a radical change,” Captain Matthew Lehman, LCS program manager, said. “We’re the first USV to [reach initial operational capability] anywhere in the Navy.”39 LCSs equipped with the MCM mission package are expected to deploy to the Seventh Fleet area of responsibility in the following year.
Although the announcement of the new battleships adds a potential high-end warship class to the fleet, the cancellation of the Constellation-class frigate program threatens to complicate the Navy’s near-term efforts to grow the fleet with an affordable ship design. Funding for Constellation-class ships was not included in either the Navy’s initial 2026 budget request or the reconciliation bill, and the program’s cost increases and lengthy construction delays made it a prime candidate for cancellation in the executive order “Modernizing Defense Acquisitions and Spurring Innovation in the Defense Industrial Base.”40 As a result, only 2 of the planned 20 frigates will be built, leaving the Fincantieri Marinette Marine shipyard without follow-on orders as the shipyard finishes Freedom-class LCS production and cancels plans to establish a second large shipyard to fulfill orders for the Constellation class.41 The end of Freedom- and Independence-class LCS production, combined with the decommissioning of early LCS hulls, and the absence of a small, low-cost combatant to complement the larger and more costly Arleigh Burke–class destroyers raises questions as to whether the Navy can fund enough ships to keep the fragile shipbuilding industrial base operating at effective rates.
Secretary Phelan’s December announcement that the Navy will pursue a new frigate based on the Coast Guard’s Legend-class national security cutter, built at HII’s Ingalls Shipbuilding, attempts to resume Legend-class production that concluded with delivery of the USCGC Calhoun (WMSL-759) in 2024 and put the Navy back on schedule for a new small surface combatant. However, rather than building HII’s Patrol Frigate 4921 variant of the Legend-class, which was bid in the Navy’s original frigate competition, the service seems to be buying the Coast Guard’s cutter straight up. HII has proposed a version of the Legend class that retains the same weapons and combat systems used in the Coast Guard variant, such as a 57-mm naval gun, the Phalanx CIWS, NULKA antiship missile decoys, and modifications to accommodate containerized mission packages, such as the Mk 70 Typhoon vertical launch system (VLS), which can launch up to four TLAMs and the extended-range version of the SM-2.42
It is unclear if this version would include any of the additional features in HII’s previously proposed patrol frigate design, such as a small 12-cell Mk 56 VLS for shorter range Evolved Seasparrow Missiles instead of the longer range SM-2, Mk 32 surface vessel torpedo tubes, a towed sonar array, a mine-detecting sonar, or an AN/SPY-1F air-defense radar. The Coast Guard version of the Legend-class cost approximately $670 million per ship in 2022, compared with $1.28 billion and $1.05 billion in 2022 dollars for the Constellation (FFG-62) and Congress (FFG-63), respectively.43
A contender to fill the void left by the frigate program’s delays and cancellation is a USV large enough to fill the fleet’s need for a low-cost vessel to complement the high- and medium-cost manned ships. Congress set aside $2.1 billion for the Modular Attack Surface Craft (MASC) program. Smaller than either the Constellation class or Legend class, the MASC is intended to be built by more U.S. shipyards faster and at a lower cost than any frigate program. Leveraging containerized weapons and sensors, the Navy is developing an unmanned vessel to serve as an adjunct magazine and a sensor platform for manned ships. Capable of carrying two 40-foot shipping containers configured with either weapons or sensors, and cruising at 25 knots for up to 2,500 nautical miles, the MASC could serve as an additional cruise and Standard Missile magazine.
The past year was one of program changes and new initiatives in the U.S. Navy. High-tempo operations in the eastern Mediterranean Sea and Red Sea concluded with the strikes against Iran’s nuclear weapon facilities. The 2025 naval buildup and blockade of sanctioned oil tankers near Venezuela and interdiction of drug-smuggling boats in the Caribbean and eastern Pacific may have peaked with the capture of Venezuela’s president. Finally, time will tell whether the shipbuilding initiatives and increased 2025 appropriations result in the new high-medium-low fleet promised under the developing Golden Fleet shipbuilding plan.
